⚡ An Enemy to Lovers Story ⚡

SPIDER&GWEN

Neo City Strategic Response Agency · Case File 001

🕷 CHAPTER 1 OF 10 🕷

The Boy Who Shoots Webs And The Girl Who Shoots Daggers

SCROLL
PAGE 1

The Boy Who Shoots Webs And The Girl Who Shoots Daggers

🕷 A rainy city. A boy on a rooftop. He looks cool. He knows it.

Neo City never slept. It blinked. It breathed. It hummed with neon lights and bad decisions at 2 AM. But it never, ever slept.

High above Street Level 9, a boy stood on the edge of a broken water tower. Rain fell in thick curtains around him, soaking his dark hair, dripping off his sharp jaw. He didn't move. He didn't blink. He just watched the city below him like it owed him something.

His name was Spider. Well — his real name was Zain. But nobody called him that anymore. Not since the accident. Not since he woke up in a hospital with silk-white threads shooting from his wrists and a doctor screaming in the hallway.

That had been a weird Tuesday.

Now he wore a black tactical suit with blue glowing lines across the chest, a hood pulled low, and an expression on his face that said: 'Try me. Please try me.'

PAGE 2

The Mission. The Target. The Part Where Things Go Wrong Immediately.

🕷 FWOOSH. THWIP.

"Target sighted," said a voice in his earpiece. "Black van. East alley. Three guards."

"Three?" Spider said, cracking his knuckles. "That's almost insulting."

"Spider. This is a recon mission. You are not supposed to engage."

He was already stepping off the water tower.

The web left his left wrist with a sound like a guitar string snapping — clean, precise, perfect. It caught the iron frame of a fire escape eight floors down. He swung low and fast, cutting through the rain like a blade through silk.

The three guards below heard nothing. Then one of them was suddenly hanging upside down from a lamppost, wrapped from chest to ankle in white web so dense it looked like he'd been gift-wrapped.

He really loved this part.

PAGE 3

Enter Gwen.

🕷 She arrives like a problem you didn't ask for.

The black van's back door cracked open in four seconds. Not because of Spider.

Spider had been about to crack it himself when the door swung open from the other side and a girl stepped out, a glowing data chip in one hand and a look on her face like she had been waiting for him specifically to show up and ruin her night.

Gwen.

In Neo City, you would have to be blind in both eyes and possibly also deaf to not know who Gwen Reyes was. Long dark hair that moved like it had its own agenda. Eyes the color of deep water — grey-green and absolutely merciless.

She was also, unfortunately, the best thief in Neo City. And she had just stolen the exact thing he had been sent to retrieve.

"Oh," she said. "It's you." "Give me the chip, Gwen." She tilted her head. Smiled. It was not a nice smile. "No."

PAGE 4

First Fight.

🕷 They are very good at arguing. Possibly too good.

She ran. He chased. Obviously.

Gwen was fast — terrifyingly fast for someone wearing heeled boots in the rain. She hit the fire escape ladder at a dead sprint, went up three floors without breaking stride, and was on the roof before he was even halfway up the building.

"Give me the chip, Gwen. Please." He said it flatly, not because he was asking politely, but because he'd been told that adding 'please' made him sound less like he was about to pin someone to a wall with silk threads.

"Hmm. What do I get?" She looked at the chip. Looked at him. "Not arrested tonight." She laughed. Bright and sharp. "That's your best offer?" "Yes." "Terrible negotiator." She pocketed the chip. "Work on it."

Then she jumped off the building.

PAGE 5

He Catches Her.

🕷 She is not grateful. This is how it begins.

For exactly one second, Spider stood at the roof edge and watched Gwen fall into the dark and thought: she has a plan, she definitely has a plan—

She did not have a plan. Her grapple hook had misfired.

He never thought about it. He just jumped.

The web left his wrist and caught her by the waist three floors from the ground. They landed on the second floor fire escape. He took the impact on his back. She ended up on top of him, her hair across his face, her elbow in his ribs.

She sat up and looked down at him. Up close, her eyes were extraordinary. Even angry, even dripping wet, she was the most extraordinary-looking person he had ever seen.

"You caught me," she said, like she was accusing him of something. "You fell." "Don't make a habit of that."

He lay in the rain and stared at the sky. He was in so much trouble.

PAGE 6

The Agency.

🕷 Spider walks in. His boss is not happy. When is she ever?

The Neo City Strategic Response Agency was three levels underground, lit by blue LED strips, and smelled like printer toner and ambition. Spider walked in at 3 AM, dripping on the polished floor.

Director Priya Singh was waiting for him. She was forty-five, had the energy of a woman who had not slept since 2019, and expressed disappointment in the tone most people used for weather reports: factual, inevitable, mildly catastrophic.

"The chip," she said. "Wasn't there." "You let Gwen Reyes walk away with classified military intelligence." "She jumped off a building. I saved her life. There was a sequence of events."

"A sequence of events." Singh set down her coffee very carefully. "Did she give you the chip at any point?" "…No." "So the mission failed."

Spider sat down. He was thinking about the way Gwen had looked down at him on that fire escape — and he was absolutely not going to mention that.

PAGE 7

New Orders. New Partner.

🕷 Spider looks like he's been told to eat something terrible.

"She's agreed to cooperate," Director Singh said, sliding a file across the desk.

Spider looked at the file. Looked at Singh. Looked back at the file.

"Gwen Reyes," he read slowly, "is agreeing to cooperate with the Agency. As a consultant. Effective immediately."

"She has the chip. The chip has data we need. She's negotiated joint access — which means she works alongside our team until the operation is resolved."

"Alongside you, specifically. She requested it."

She requested it.

"She said, and I'm quoting directly, 'Send the web boy. At least he's entertaining.'"

He stared at the ceiling for a long moment. The LED strips hummed. The universe offered him no comfort.

"When does she start?" "She's already in the briefing room."

PAGE 8

Briefing Room.

🕷 Gwen is already there. She's been waiting. She's enjoying this.

She was sitting on the edge of the briefing table — not in a chair, on the table, legs crossed, jacket dry, hair somehow perfect — eating a granola bar like she owned the building.

"You look terrible," she said. "You stole classified intelligence." "And I'm giving it back, slowly and with conditions."

"The chip," she said, pulling out a holographic display, "has a weapons manifest. But look at the routing codes." She expanded the display. "This shipment isn't coming from outside Neo City. It's already here. It's been here for three months. And whoever is moving it — they have agency clearance."

Spider went very still. "Someone inside," he said. "Someone inside," she confirmed.

For the first time all night, she was not smiling. And he was not arguing. And something shifted between them — not warmth, not yet — but something like the careful opening of a door.

PAGE 9

Late Night. A Truce Is Not Made.

🕷 But it is strongly implied.

By 4 AM, they had covered three walls with data and eliminated eleven dead ends. Gwen worked fast and methodically — faster than he expected, which annoyed him.

"You went to Crane Academy," she said, not looking up. "For two years. Then the ability manifested and that was the end of that." "I heard about the incident. The lab explosion." "Half true. There was a lab. There was a spider. The explosion came later when I panicked and webbed the entire chemistry department to the ceiling."

She laughed. A real laugh — surprised out of her. Softer than the sharp rooftop version.

"And you?" he said. "How does the best thief in Neo City end up with military intel?" "I was used, Spider. I don't like being used."

He studied her face. She was telling the truth. "Okay," he said simply. "Okay? That's it? You believe me?" "Yes."

The room was quiet. Not an uncomfortable quiet.

PAGE 10

Dawn. The Case Is Real.

🕷 The enemy is inside. The team is stuck with each other.

Light crept through the ventilation grates — pale and grey, the honest 5 AM kind.

"Three months," Gwen said quietly. "Whoever this is has been doing this for three months and no one caught them."

"We're going to need to trust each other," she said. "Do you trust me?" "I trust that you'll catch me if I fall off another building," she said. "That's a start."

"I don't work with partners." "I know. I read your file." "You read my—" "Three classified incidents. No registered address. Calls himself Spider." She tilted her head. "You're interesting, Zain."

He went very still at the sound of his real name. Nobody said that name.

She was already walking toward the door, smile like a lit match. "See you tomorrow, web boy. Don't be late."

Spider stood in the empty room as dawn crept in, thinking very carefully about absolutely nothing in particular.

🕷 🕷 🕷

— End of Chapter 1 —

The Market, The Gunfight, and The Moment He Almost Said Something Nice
